title: Metrosideros lanceolata
intro: Metrosideros lanceolata, commonly known as the New Zealand Christmas tree or Pōhutukawa, is a species of flowering plant native to New Zealand. The leaves and bark of M. lanceolata have been traditionally used in Maori medicine, and research has identified several health benefits, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which may help to protect against diseases such as cancer and cardiovascular disease. The bioactive constituents of M. lanceolata include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, M. lanceolata is often prepared as a tea, tincture, or extract, and is used to treat a range of conditions, including digestive issues, fever, and respiratory infections.
taxonomy: Metrosideros lanceolata, also known as the New Zealand Christmas tree or Pōhutukawa, is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the myrtle family (Myrtaceae). This family is a group of flowering plants that includes around 130 genera and more than 3,000 species. The Myrtaceae family is part of the larger order Myrtales, which also includes other families like the Thymelaeaceae and the Penaeaceae. The order Myrtales is further grouped into the clade Rosids, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Metrosideros lanceolata's classification highlights its unique position within the plant kingdom.
uses: Metrosideros lanceolata, commonly known as the New Zealand Christmas tree, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes.
